coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots as sensitive “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor for tetracycline and Al3+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xinxin
Xu, Yi
Wang, Zhongrui
Huang, Xiaojie
Xiao, Guojing
Yang, Guang
Feng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Novel bifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared through solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and citric acid monohydrate (CA) as precursors. The N-CDs displayed uniform size distribution, excellent photostability and bright fluorescence emission with the fluorescence quantum yield of 24 %. The N-CD-based fluorescence “on–off-on” sensing platform exhibited rapid, selective and sensitive detection of TCs (tetracycline hydrochloride and doxycycline) and Al3+. The fluorescence at 472 nm can be quenched by TCs through the internal filtration effect (IFE) and can also be recovered with the addition of Al3+ by the stronger affinity between Al3+ and TCs than that of CDs. The fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and high sensitivity toward TCs and Al3+ detection with low detection limits for tetracycline hydrochloride: 91.8 nM, doxycycline: 78 nM and Al3+: 163.5 nM. Moreover, the proposed sensing platform is utilized for detecting TCs and Al3+ in tap water and milk samples. In addition, the N-CD probe can be used for cell imaging based on its low toxicity and good biocompatibility, implying the potential applications in the fields of biomarkers and bioanalysis in vivo imaging studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Tetracycline antibiotics
Al3+
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs and Al3+.
Tcs detection based on N-CDs as fluorophores
Detection of TCs in real samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the N-CDs (Inset are HRTEM image and size distribution histograms).
(a) XPS spectrum and the high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s of the N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum, fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (inset photos of the N-CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with different excitation wavelength.

(a) FL spectra of the N-CDs upon adding different amount of TCH and (b) the linear relationship between FL intensity at 472 nm and the concentrations of TCH. (c) FL spectra of the N-CDs upon adding different amount of DOX and (d) the linear relationship between FL intensity at 472 nm and the concentrations of DOX.
Schematic illustration of preparation of N-CDs and detection of TCs and Al3+.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots as sensitive “on–off-on” fluorescent sensor for tetracycline and Al3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study describes a facile strategy to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and citric acid monohydrate (CA) as precursors. The N-CD-based fluorescence “on–off-on” sensing platform exhibited rapid, selective and sensitive detection of TCs (tetracycline hydrochloride and doxycycline) and Al3+. In addition, the N-CD probe can be used for cell imaging based on its low toxicity and good biocompatibility, implying the potential applications in the fields of biomarkers and bioanalysis in vivo imaging studies.

Novel bifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared through solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and citric acid monohydrate (CA) as precursors. The N-CDs displayed uniform size distribution, excellent photostability and bright fluorescence emission with the fluorescence quantum yield of 24 %. The N-CD-based fluorescence “on–off-on” sensing platform exhibited rapid, selective and sensitive detection of TCs (tetracycline hydrochloride and doxycycline) and Al3+. The fluorescence at 472 nm can be quenched by TCs through the internal filtration effect (IFE) and can also be recovered with the addition of Al3+ by the stronger affinity between Al3+ and TCs than that of CDs. The fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and high sensitivity toward TCs and Al3+ detection with low detection limits for tetracycline hydrochloride: 91.8 nM, doxycycline: 78 nM and Al3+: 163.5 nM. Moreover, the proposed sensing platform is utilized for detecting TCs and Al3+ in tap water and milk samples. In addition, the N-CD probe can be used for cell imaging based on its low toxicity and good biocompatibility, implying the potential applications in the fields of biomarkers and bioanalysis in vivo imaging studies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), well-known kinds of broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agents that were first synthesized in the 1940 s and displayed excellent antibacterial abilities, have been widely used for treating bacterial infections in animals and humans [1]. However, due to their merits of low cost and excellent properties in the treatment of diseases and promotion of animal growth, heavy and excessive use of TC antibiotics including tetracycline (TCH), doxycycline (DOX) and oxytetracycline (OTC) has led to accumulation in foods such as milk, meat and eggs, water samples and finally accumulated in the human body through consumption. Thus promoting bacterial resistance and leading to damage to the liver, kidney and teeth and reduces human immunity [2–4]. Recently, several conventional analytical techniques have been developed for detecting TCs, including high-efficiency liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis (CE), mass spectrometry (MS), colorimetric or electrochemical analysis and so on [5–9]. However, the above methods are faced with several disadvantages such as requiring sophisticated and expensive devices, time-consuming sample pretreatments and technical skills, limiting their applications in real-time detection.

Recently, fluorescence-based analytical techniques have gained increasing attention owing to their easy operation, fast response time, high sensitivity and low-cost characteristics for the detection of analytes [10–15].To date, many fluorescence probes based on metal nanoclusters, lanthanide complex-based fluorescent materials, quantum dots and polymer dots have been designed for the detection of TCs [16–22]. Unfortunately, several drawbacks still exist for the above fluorescent sensors, such as complex synthesis, higher toxicity and lower luminescence quantum yield. Therefore, a new fluorescent sensor for monitoring TCs with simple, inexpensive, fast and efficient properties is highly desired, and the development of new fluorescent sensors for the detection of antibiotics is of great significance in both scientific and technical aspects.

As a novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nano-fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have shown tremendous application potential in the sensor field due to their featuring outstanding advantages, including simple and rapid preparation, low production cost, bright fluorescence, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and high photostability [23–28]. Several fluorescent probes have been designed for the detection of TCs based on CDs, and nitrogen element was believed to be a quintessential dopant and had been widely used to enhance the fluorescence property of CDs. Fan et al constructed cerium–nitrogen codoped CDs (Ce–N–CDs) through a hydrothermal method for the tracing of doxycycline with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.25 µM [29]. Lan et al prepared S,N-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) for the detection of tetracycline, doxycycline and oxytetracycline with LOD of 0.25, 0.39 and 0.26 μM [2]. Mg/N codoped CDs with 33.05 % quantum yield were prepared by Wang’s group, and TC quenched the fluorescence of CDs with a LOD of 0.79 ng.mL −1 [11]. However, the above fluorescent sensors are concentrated in the short wavelength emission region. To realize CDs with longwavelength emission, Cheng et al designed N, S-doped carbon dots by a typical hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs show bright orange fluorescence (FL) emission and can be quenched by tetracycline antibiotics through the inner filter effect [30]. Meanwhile, Yan and coworkers synthesized yellow-green luminescent CDs based on amine-aldehyde condensation, and the FL could be quenched in the presence of tetracycline hydrochloride with a LOD of 6.0 μM [31]. Recently, ratiometric fluorescence sensors have been shown to possess two different wavelength emissions under the same excitation wavelength, which endows the probe with high sensitivity and identifiability. Pu et al designed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (denoted as Cu-CDs–COOH–Eu) for detecting TC through the IFE effect with a LOD of 36.1 nM [10]. Hu et al designed a Mg,NCDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP fluorescent sensor for the visual smart detection of oxytetracycline by the synergistic effect of IFE and AE [9]. However, most of the above sensors only focus on the fluorescence “on–off” strategy, and whether the quenched system can be recovered has not been further researched. Liu et al prepared blue emitting CDs based on lysine as a precursor by microwave method, and detected seven kinds of TCs by the IFE effect. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of the quenched probe can be recovered after adding Al3+ [32]. Shuang et al designed a FL “on–off-on” CD-based probe for the detection of TCs and Al3+. This sensor displayed yellow–emitting properties, and the FL of y-CDs can be directly quenched by TCs through the mechanism of IFE. Interestingly, Al3+ can partly recover the fluorescence owing to the stronger affinity towards TCs than that of CDs [33]. Despite the above efforts, few works have been reported based on CDs for the detection of TCs and Al3+, and strategies with fluorescence “on–off-on” need to be further researched.

Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and citric acid monohydrate (CA), and the synthetic route is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The N-CDs display a uniform structure, excellent photostability, bright FL emission, low toxicity and good biocompatibility and could be used for cell imaging. More strikingly, the as-prepared N-CD-based sensing platform was utilized for detecting TCs and Al3+ through a fluorescence “on–off-on” process. The TCs can quench the N-CDs through the IFE effects, and the Al3+ can partly recover the fluorescence owing to the stronger affinity toward TCs than that of CDs. Meanwhile, the N-CDs-based fluorescent sensor could quantitative analysis TCs and Al3+ in real samples.

Melamine and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The standards of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and doxycycline (DOX) were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Other antibiotics, amino acids and glucose were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). MgSO4 and other organic salts were provided by the Kermel Chemical Reagent Development Center (Tianjin, China). Deionized water (18.25 MΩ), solvents and reagents (analytical grade) were used in all experiments. All experiments were performed at least three measurements.

FL spectra were recorded through a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer, the excitation wavelength (λex) was 380 nm. UV-2550 PC spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU) was selected to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. An FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) was utilized for testing the fluorescence lifetime. Transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2, Netherlands) was used for the collection of TEM images. A Spectrum 400 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for recording Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Al Kaline, 1486.6 eV), respectively. The Raman spectrum was recorded by a laser confocal micro Raman Spectrometer (DXR2).

N-CDs were obtained through solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and CA. Briefly, melamine (0.895 g, 7.1 mM) and CA (0.987 g, 4.7 mM) were added in a tubular furnace, and then heated to 170 °C under argon gas protecting at a rate of 10 °C /min and kept heating for 1.5 h. The product was naturally cooled to room temperature and dissolved in 200 ml distilled water. The insoluble product was filtered out and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, a 0.22 μM filter membrane was selected to purify the supernatant and freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain yellow solids for further use.

The relative quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was measured by quinine sulfate (dispersed in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54 %) as a reference. The calculated equation as follows:
QY =
Q R
I /
I R
OD R
/ OD
n
2
/
n
R
2

QY represents the fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs, QR stands for the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate (φ = 0.546, refractive index n = 1.33), I is the measured integrated FL intensity of N-CDs, and IR represents the measured integrated FL intensity of quinine sulfate. OD and ODR stand for absorbance density of N-CDs and quinine sulfate (often less than 0.05), respectively. n is the refractive index. All the data of the solution were obtained under the same specification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs and Al3+.
ce:section.ce:para: Different amounts of TCs were added to 2 ml of N-CDs solution (0.36 mg/mL), and kept the mixture reacted for 1 min. The FL spectra were recorded with the excitation wavelength was 380 nm. For the detection of Al3+, different amounts of Al3+ were added to N-CDs (0.36 mg·mL−1) + TCs (TCH for 167 μM and DOX for 136 μM) system. Both the excitation and emission slit widths were 5 nm. The FL intensities at 472 nm were utilized for fitting the curve to the.

Tap water was acquired in this laboratory and filtered by a 0.22 µm membrane. Samples of milk were obtained from supermarkets. First, 10 ml of 8 % (W/V) trichloroacetic acid was added to 25 ml of milk sample to remove the protein. After 15 min of ultrasonic treatment, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, filtered with a 0.22 μM microporous membrane and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. The detection process was the same as the section of 2.5.

HeLa cells were inoculated in a 96-well cell culture plate (1.0 × 103 cells/well) and incubated overnight. Then, a certain concentration of carbon dots was added for further culture for 24 h. Then washed the above cells with PBS for 3 times and added 20 μL MTT solution to each well. After 4 h of incubation, the culture was terminated and added 150 μL DMSO to each well as a substitute solution. The absorbance was determined at 490 nm by the colorimetric method.

N-CDs with bright fluorescence and excellent water solubility were prepared through solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and CA. The TEM image in Fig. 1 indicates a spherical morphology for N-CDs with an average diameter of 2.2 nm without any obvious aggregation. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1) shows evident lattice fringes of 0.22 nm, which is attributed to the plane (100) of graphite carbon [34–36]. In addition, XRD pattern in Fig. S1 shows a wide peak at 23.4°, suggesting the disorder pattern of CDs during the introduction of N- and O-containing groups [37–39]. Raman spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. S2) shows two typical peaks in 1350 and 1587 cm−1, standing for the D and G band, respectively. The surface composition and chemical state of N-CDs were investigated by FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. S3, two typical absorption peaks centered at 3372 and 3210 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, respectively. The typical absorbance peak at 1277 cm−1 refers to the stretching vibrations of CO. The characteristic peaks at 1670, 1552 and 1178 cm−1 refer to the stretching vibrations of –CO-NH, CN and CN, respectively. Meanwhile, XPS spectra were also analyzed to deeply understand the chemical structure of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, C1s, O1s and N1s were clearly observed at 285 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV, with the percentages of 49.19 %, 16.08 % and 34.72 %, respectively. The typical C1s spectrum has three obvious peaks at 284.2, 284.5, 285.4, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to CC, CN/CO, and N-CO groups, respectively (Fig. 2b). The typical peaks for N1s at 398.8 eV refer to CN group (Fig. 2c). There are three peaks at 530.9, 531.9 and 532.5 eV in the O1s spectrum, which are related to OC-OH, CO and CO bands (Fig. 2d), respectively. The XPS analysis data are consistent with the FT-IR data, and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of N-CDs (Fig. S4) also confirmed the existence of C, N and O elements. All above results demonstrated the successful synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots.

The optical characteristics of the N-CDs were researched by UV–vis and FL spectral analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, two obvious absorbance peaks at 204 nm and 235 nm are assigned to the π-π* transition of CC in the core of N-CDs [40]. The pale yellow N-CDs solutions can emit bright blue luminescence with 365 nm UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. 3a), and the maximum emission wavelength (λem) of N-CDs is located at 472 nm at the optimal excitation of 380 nm (λex). Meanwhile, as the wavelength of λex changes from 320 nm to 420 nm, the wavelength of λem demonstrates a typical excitation-dependent emission property with an obvious redshift, which was because of the quantum confinement effect and different emission traps (Fig. 3b) [41]. The QY of N-CDs is calculated to be 24 %, indicating the competent for sensing applications. Meanwhile, photostability of N-CDs was researched by monitoring the FL intensity at 472 nm with prolonging the irradiation time of 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. S5a, the FL intensities of N-CDs at 472 nm remain nearly unchanged after 1 h of exposure, thus confirming the good photostability of N-CDs. Increasing the concentration of NaCl also has little effect on the FL intensities of the N-CDs (Fig. S5b). Fig. S5c shows the excellent thermostability of FL intensities of the N-CDs, and the FL intensity showed no significant change as the temperature increased from 25 °C to 60 °C. Moreover, the FL intensity at 472 nm decreased with decreasing pH value under 8, and reached a maximum at pH = 8. With the increase of pH value (pH > 8), a slight FL intensity decrease could be observed, implying the stability for N-CDs, and the pH influence can be neglected when the pH > 8 (Fig. S5d). All the above results demonstrate that the as-prepared N-CDs can be utilized as a candidate material for designing fluorescent sensors.

The bright fluorescence emission property allows the N-CDs to be utilized for cell imaging. Herein, HeLa cells were used as a model to evaluate the vitro cell toxicity for N-CDs. The results demonstrated a low cytotoxicity for N-CDs even under a high concentration (1 mg mL−1), and the cell viability was still higher than 93 % (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S7, obvious blue fluorescence can be noticed for the cells after cultured with N-CDs for 6 h, which indicates potential applications in bioimaging based on N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tcs detection based on N-CDs as fluorophores
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs prepared above can be used as promising fluorescent chemical-sensors for TCs detection. As shown in Fig. 4 a, upon excitation at 380 nm, the N-CDs demonstrate strong emission at 472 nm, the initial fluorescence of N-CDs can be quenched by TCH, and the FL intensity gradually decreases with increasing TCH amount. The FL intensity at 472 nm versus TCH amount displays two good linear relationships in the range of 0–87.5 μM (R2 = 0.984) and 87.5–166.6 μM (R2 = 0.996) of TCH, and the limit of detection (LOD) of TCH is 91.8 nM based on 3σ/K (σ represents the standard deviation between the corrected blank signal and seven parallel measurements, and K refers the slope of the calibration curve) (Fig. 4b). The results meet the maximum acceptable levels defined by China and the European Union [29]. The quenching efficiency((F0-F)/F0) of TCH can be as high as 76 % when TCH increases to 166 μM, and the quenching efficiency versus TCH displays two linear relationships at concentrations ranging from 0 to 87.5 μM (R2 = 0.984) and from 87.5 to 166.6 μM(R2 = 0.996) (Fig. S8). Meanwhile, the N-CDs also display an obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon after the addition of DOX, and the FL intensity at 472 nm gradually decreased with increasing DOX content (Fig. 4c and d). The LOD of DOX is 72 nM (S/N = 3), and the quenching efficiency can be as high as 79 %. Two linear relationships were also observed between quenching efficiency and DOX amount in the range from 0 to 57 μM (R2 = 0.994) and from 57 to 136 μM(R2 = 0.976) (Fig. S9). To investigate the reproducibility of as-prepared N-CDs sensors, several measurements were carried out with the addition of 100 μM TCH or DOX. As illustrated in Fig. S10, N-CDs fluorescent sensor displayed good reproducibility with a relative standard deviation of 7.1 % for TCH and 6.7 % for DOX, respectively. All of the above results indicate that the fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs can be used to accurately quantify TCH and DOX.

To further elucidate the selectivity of N-CDs for TCH and DOX detection, the FL intensities of N-CDs were recorded with adding a variety of other antibiotics (ampicillin, oxytetracycline, penicillin G, streptomycin, erythrocin, chloramphenicol, thiamphenicol), interference analysis (cefotaxime, BSA, lysine, aspartic acid, glutathione, glutamic acid, cysteine) and other metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+) at 100 μM. Only TCH and DOX can cause remarkable FL quenching and other interferers had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, which demonstrates the outstanding selectivity of the N-CDs fluorescent sensor for the detection of TCH and DOX (Fig. S11). In addition, a comparison of other prepared methods for the detection of tetracyclines is summarized in Table S1, this work described exceptional advantages in terms of linear range as well as comparable limit of detection and quantum yields.

As shown in Fig. S12, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of TCH and DOX are observed to partially overlap with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs, thus the possible quenching principle of N-CDs by TCs may result from the inner filter effect (IFE) process. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs remained unchanged with increasing amounts of TCs (Fig. S13), which indicates a static quenching process by IFE.

More strikingly, the FL emission for quenched fluorescence of the N-CDs sensor with the treatment of TCs can be recovered in the presence of Al3+. As shown in Fig. S14a, the FL intensity at 472 nm gradually recovered with increasing Al3+ amount, and the maximum recovery of the N-CDs + TCH system reached approximately 77.5 % under excitation at 380 nm. Meanwhile, the linear regression equation of lg(F/F0) versus Al3+ concentration is lg(F/F0) = 0.00237C + 0.0119 (R2 = 0.992), and the LOD for Al3+ was calculated to be 163.5 nM (S/N = 3) (Fig. S14b). Moreover, the FL emission for N-CDs + DOX can also be recovered after adding Al3+, and the maximum recovery of the N-CDs + DOX system can reach approximately 70.9 % (Fig. S14c). The linear regression equation of lg(F/F0) versus Al3+ concentration is lg(F/F0) = 0.00244C-0.0311 (R2 = 0.995), and the LOD for Al3+ was calculated to be 146.6 nM (S/N = 3), which is comparable to earlier reported works (Fig. S14d) and the recovery of N-CDs + TCH system exhibited good reproducibility with a relative standard deviation of 3.5 % (Fig. S15). In addition, compared with other metal ions, only Al3+ can realize FL intensity recovery for the N-CDs + TCH system (Fig. S16). All the above data demonstrate that the N-CDs + TCH system can be utilized for ideal sensors for the detection of Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real samples.
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility and practicality of the N-CDs based fluorescent sensor, the standards of TCs (GBW) with different amounts in tap water and milk samples were determined. As shown in Table S2, for tap water samples, the recoveries ranged from 97.45 % to 98.93 % for TCH and from 96.43 % to 100.6 % for DOX. The recoveries for TCH and DOX in milk samples ranged from 96.50 % to 104.0 % and from 96.53 % to 99.31 %, respectively (Table S3 ). Furthermore, different amounts of Al3+ were added to the N-CDs + TCH and N-CDs + DOX systems, as shown in Table S4 and S5, the recoveries for Al3+ ranged from 98.80 % to 101.3 % in tap water and from 96.45 % to 99.75 % in milk samples for the N-CDs + TCH system. For N-CDs + DOX system, the recoveries for Al3+ ranged from 98.33 % to 104.3 % in tap water and 97.55 % to 100.5 % in milk samples. The results are satisfactory and clearly verify the usefulness of the N-CDs-based sensing platform in real sample analysis.

In summary, bright blue fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized by solid-phase thermal treatment of melamine and citric acid monohydrate (CA) as precursors. The N-CDs have a uniform size distribution, excellent water solubility, good fluorescence property and low toxicity, and the QY of N-CDs is as high as 24 %. Therefore, a sensitive sensor has been established for detecting TCs and Al3+ based on the FL “on–off-on” mechanism. In the presence of TCs (TCH and DOX), an obvious FL quenching phenomenon can be observed based on IFE, and the FL emission can be recovered in the presence of Al3+, thus confirming the stronger affinity between Al3+ and TCs than that of CDs. The N-CDs-based sensor exhibits sensitivity and selectivity for TCs and Al3+ detection and can be used for the rapid detection of TCs and Al3+ in real samples. Moreover, the N-CDs can be utilized for intracellular bioimaging and thus possess great potential applications as biomarkers in vivo imaging studies.
The datasets used or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
Not applicable.
The article does not contain any experiments with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Consent to participate was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Consent for publication was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Xinxin Ji: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yi Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Supervision. Zhongrui Wang: Supervision. Xiaojie Huang: Writing – review & editing. Guojing Xiao: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Guang Yang: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding. Wei Feng: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2020E007), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021BU05), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 51803021) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018 M641790).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108290.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline detection
Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.

Environ. Pollut.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New carbon dots based on glycerol and urea and its application in the determination of tetracycline in urine samples
